Apple___Apple_scab.description: Apple scab is a fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis. It affects leaves, fruit, and twigs, leading to dark, olive-green to black lesions.
symptoms: Olive-green to dark brown velvety spots on leaves
Scabby, cracked lesions on fruit surface
Premature leaf drop in severe cases
Distorted or stunted fruit growth
prevention: Remove and destroy fallen infected leaves
Prune trees to improve air circulation
Apply fungicide sprays before rainy season
Plant resistant apple varieties like Liberty or Enterprise
Maintain proper tree spacing

Apple___Black_rot.description: Black rot is caused by the fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa. It causes leaf spots, fruit rot, and cankers on branches.
symptoms: Purple-bordered brown spots on leaves (frogeye leaf spot)
Brown to black rotting on fruit, starting at blossom end
Sunken cankers on branches and trunk
Mummified fruit remains on tree
prevention: Remove mummified fruit and dead wood from trees
Prune out cankers during dormant season
Apply fungicide at pink bud and petal fall stages
Maintain good orchard sanitation
Avoid wounding fruit during harvest

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: Cedar apple rust is caused by the fungus Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. It requires both apple and cedar/juniper as alternate hosts.
symptoms: Bright yellow-orange spots on upper leaf surface
Small black dots in center of leaf spots
Tube-like projections on leaf undersides
Deformed or spotted fruit
prevention: Remove nearby cedar and juniper trees if possible
Plant rust-resistant apple varieties
Apply fungicide from pink bud through petal fall
Scout for galls on cedars in early spring and remove them
Maintain proper tree nutrition for stronger resistance

Apple___healthy.description: The apple plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Continue regular monitoring for pests and diseases
Maintain proper irrigation and nutrition schedule
Prune regularly for good air circulation
Apply dormant oil spray in early spring
Practice integrated pest management (IPM)

Blueberry___healthy.description: The blueberry plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Maintain acidic soil pH (4.5-5.5)
Mulch with pine needles or wood chips
Ensure adequate drainage
Prune old canes annually
Monitor for spotted wing drosophila

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew on cherry is caused by Podosphaera clandestina. It creates a white powdery coating on leaves and fruit.
symptoms: White powdery coating on leaf surfaces
Leaf curling and distortion
Stunted shoot growth
White patches on fruit surface
prevention: Ensure good air circulation through pruning
Avoid overhead irrigation
Remove and destroy infected shoots
Apply fungicide at first sign of disease
Plant resistant varieties when available

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.description: The cherry plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Maintain proper spacing between trees
Regular pruning for air circulation
Monitor for brown rot and leaf spot
Apply dormant spray in late winter
Keep area around trees clean

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: Gray leaf spot is caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. It is one of the most significant yield-limiting diseases of corn worldwide.
symptoms: Small, rectangular, gray to tan lesions on leaves
Lesions run parallel to leaf veins
Lesions may merge, blighting entire leaves
Lower leaves affected first, progressing upward
prevention: Rotate crops â€” avoid planting corn after corn
Use resistant hybrids
Till crop residues to reduce inoculum
Ensure adequate plant spacing
Apply foliar fungicide at tasseling stage

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: Common rust of corn is caused by Puccinia sorghi. It produces small, reddish-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces.
symptoms: Small, round to elongated reddish-brown pustules
Pustules appear on both upper and lower leaf surfaces
Severe infection causes leaf yellowing and death
Pustules turn dark brown to black as they mature
prevention: Plant rust-resistant hybrids
Early planting to avoid peak rust season
Monitor fields regularly, especially during cool, humid weather
Apply fungicide at first sign if susceptible hybrid
Maintain balanced fertilization

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: Northern leaf blight is caused by Exserohilum turcicum. It causes long, cigar-shaped grayish-green lesions on corn leaves.
symptoms: Long, elliptical (cigar-shaped) grayish-green to tan lesions
Lesions 2-15 cm long on leaves
Lower leaves affected first
Severe cases cause premature leaf death
prevention: Plant resistant hybrids
Rotate with non-host crops (soybeans, wheat)
Till infected crop residue
Avoid overhead irrigation
Apply fungicide at tasseling if disease pressure is high

Corn_(maize)___healthy.description: The corn plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Practice crop rotation
Maintain proper plant spacing and nutrition
Scout regularly for pests and diseases
Use quality seed from reliable sources
Manage weeds to reduce disease pressure

Grape___Black_rot.description: Black rot is caused by Guignardia bidwellii. It attacks all green parts of the vine and is most destructive to fruit.
symptoms: Circular reddish-brown spots on leaves
Small black pycnidia in leaf spots
Fruit turns brown, then black and shriveled (mummified)
Cankers on shoots and tendrils
prevention: Remove mummified fruits and infected plant material
Prune vines properly for air circulation
Apply fungicide from bud break to veraison
Ensure good drainage in vineyard
Destroy crop residues after harvest

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: Esca is a complex fungal trunk disease caused by multiple fungi. It causes distinctive tiger-stripe patterns on leaves.
symptoms: Interveinal chlorosis giving tiger-stripe pattern on leaves
Dark spots on berries (measles-like)
Sudden vine collapse (apoplexy) in severe cases
Wood decay visible in cross-section of trunk
pesticides: Trichoderma-based biological products for wound protection
prevention: Avoid large pruning wounds â€” make clean cuts
Apply wound protectant paste after pruning
Remove and burn severely infected vines
Retrain new shoots from below infected area
Maintain vine health through balanced nutrition

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: Grape leaf blight (Isariopsis leaf spot) is caused by Pseudocercospora vitis. It causes brown necrotic spots on grape leaves.
symptoms: Small dark brown spots on leaves
Spots enlarge with dark margins
Premature defoliation in severe cases
Reduced vine vigor
prevention: Remove and destroy infected leaves
Ensure good air circulation through canopy management
Avoid overhead irrigation
Apply preventive fungicide sprays
Maintain balanced vine nutrition

Grape___healthy.description: The grape vine appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Regular canopy management and pruning
Monitor for downy and powdery mildew
Maintain balanced fertilization
Ensure proper drainage
Practice integrated pest management

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: Huanglongbing (HLB) or citrus greening is caused by Candidatus Liberibacter bacteria, spread by the Asian citrus psyllid. It is the most devastating citrus disease worldwide.
symptoms: Asymmetric blotchy mottling on leaves (unlike nutrient deficiency which is symmetric)
Lopsided, small, bitter fruit that stays green
Yellow shoots (yellow dragon symptom)
Twig dieback and overall tree decline
pesticides: No cure available â€” manage the vector (psyllid)
Dimethoate 30% EC (2 mL/L) for psyllid control
Neem oil 1500 ppm (5 mL/L) as deterrent
prevention: Use certified disease-free nursery plants
Control Asian citrus psyllid populations aggressively
Remove and destroy infected trees promptly
Plant windbreaks to reduce psyllid movement
Regular scouting for psyllids and symptoms

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot of peach is caused by Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni. It affects leaves, fruit, and twigs.
symptoms: Small, angular, water-soaked spots on leaves
Spots turn purple-brown, may fall out creating shot-hole appearance
Sunken, dark spots on fruit
Cankers on twigs
pesticides: Copper hydroxide 77% WP (2 g/L) â€” early season only
prevention: Plant resistant varieties
Avoid overhead irrigation
Provide good air circulation through pruning
Apply copper sprays at leaf fall and before bud break
Use windbreaks to reduce rain splash spread

Peach___healthy.description: The peach plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Regular pruning for open canopy
Monitor for peach leaf curl in spring
Apply dormant fungicide spray
Control borers and other pests
Maintain balanced nutrition

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot of pepper is caused by Xanthomonas species. It causes leaf spots, fruit spots, and defoliation.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked, dark green spots on leaves
Spots enlarge, turn brown with yellow halos
Raised, scab-like spots on fruit
Severe defoliation exposing fruit to sunscald
prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds
Practice crop rotation (2-3 years)
Avoid overhead irrigation
Remove infected plant debris
Use resistant varieties when available

Pepper,_bell___healthy.description: The bell pepper plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Maintain consistent watering
Support plants with stakes or cages
Monitor for aphids and whiteflies
Mulch to maintain soil moisture
Rotate crops annually

Potato___Early_blight.description: Early blight is caused by Alternaria solani. It produces characteristic concentric ring (target-like) lesions on potato leaves.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target board pattern)
Spots appear on older, lower leaves first
Yellowing around lesions
Premature defoliation reducing tuber yield
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed tubers
Practice 2-3 year crop rotation
Maintain adequate plant nutrition (especially potassium)
Remove infected plant debris after harvest
Apply protective fungicide sprays during humid weather

Potato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is caused by Phytophthora infestans â€” the same pathogen that caused the Irish Potato Famine. It is the most destructive potato disease.
symptoms: Water-soaked, pale green spots on leaf tips and edges
Spots rapidly enlarge, turning dark brown to black
White mold growth on underside of leaves in humid conditions
Tubers develop brown, granular rot
pesticides: Metalaxyl 8% + Mancozeb 64% WP (Ridomil Gold â€” 2.5 g/L)
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed tubers
Plant resistant varieties (Kufri Jyoti, Kufri Badshah)
Destroy volunteer potato plants
Avoid overhead irrigation
Apply preventive fungicide before rainy season
Hill up soil around plants to protect tubers

Potato___healthy.description: The potato plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Use certified seed tubers
Practice crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops
Maintain proper drainage
Monitor for Colorado potato beetle
Hill up soil to prevent tuber greening

Raspberry___healthy.description: The raspberry plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Remove spent canes after fruiting
Maintain good air circulation
Use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry
Mulch to suppress weeds and retain moisture
Scout for spotted wing drosophila

Soybean___healthy.description: The soybean plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Use treated, high-quality seed
Rotate with corn or other non-legume crops
Monitor for aphids and soybean cyst nematode
Maintain proper row spacing
Scout fields regularly during growing season

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew on squash is primarily caused by Podosphaera xanthii. It creates a distinctive white powdery coating on leaves.
symptoms: White, talcum-like powdery patches on upper leaf surface
Patches spread to cover entire leaf
Leaves yellow and dry up
Reduced fruit size and quality
pesticides: Sulphur 80% WP (3 g/L) â€” avoid in hot weather
prevention: Plant resistant varieties
Ensure proper plant spacing for air flow
Avoid overhead watering
Remove and destroy severely infected leaves
Apply preventive sprays when conditions favor disease

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: Leaf scorch is caused by the fungus Diplocarpon earlianum. It causes purple blotches on strawberry leaves.
symptoms: Small, irregular purple spots on leaf upper surface
Spots enlarge and merge, creating scorched appearance
Leaf margins turn brown and curl upward
Severe defoliation weakens plants
prevention: Use certified disease-free transplants
Renovate strawberry beds after harvest
Remove old infected leaves
Ensure proper drainage and spacing
Apply fungicide during wet weather

Strawberry___healthy.description: The strawberry plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Use certified disease-free transplants
Mulch with straw to keep fruit clean
Maintain proper plant spacing
Remove runners to maintain plant vigor
Monitor for spider mites and slugs

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot on tomato is caused by Xanthomonas species. It causes leaf spots, fruit spots, and defoliation.
symptoms: Small, dark, water-soaked, greasy spots on leaves
Spots may have yellow halos
Raised, scab-like bumps on fruit
Severe defoliation leading to sunscald on fruit
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed and transplants
Rotate crops â€” avoid planting tomato after pepper or tomato
Avoid overhead irrigation
Stake plants to improve air circulation
Remove and destroy infected debris

Tomato___Early_blight.description: Early blight on tomato is caused by Alternaria solani. It produces concentric ring (bull's-eye) lesions on older leaves.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target pattern) on lower leaves
Yellow halos around spots
Stems may develop dark, sunken cankers
Premature defoliation from bottom up
prevention: Use resistant varieties
Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash
Water at the base of plants, not overhead
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Practice 2-3 year crop rotation

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight on tomato is caused by Phytophthora infestans. It can destroy an entire crop within days under favorable conditions.
symptoms: Large, irregular, water-soaked grayish-green spots on leaves
White fuzzy mold on underside of leaves in humid conditions
Dark brown to black lesions on stems
Firm, dark, greasy spots on fruit
prevention: Use resistant varieties (Roma, Mountain Magic)
Avoid overhead irrigation
Ensure good air circulation â€” stake and prune plants
Remove and destroy all infected plant material
Apply preventive fungicide during cool, wet weather
Do not compost infected plants

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: Leaf mold is caused by Passalora fulva (formerly Cladosporium fulvum). It thrives in warm, humid greenhouse conditions.
symptoms: Pale greenish-yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to grayish-purple fuzzy mold on lower leaf surface
Leaves curl, wither, and drop
Usually starts on lower leaves and moves upward
prevention: Improve ventilation in greenhouses
Reduce humidity below 85%
Avoid overhead watering
Remove infected leaves promptly
Use resistant varieties
Space plants properly for air circulation

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Septoria leaf spot is caused by Septoria lycopersici. It is one of the most common foliage diseases of tomatoes.
symptoms: Numerous small (2-3 mm) circular spots with dark margins
Spots have grayish-white centers with tiny black dots (pycnidia)
Lower leaves affected first
Heavy spotting leads to yellowing and leaf drop
prevention: Remove infected lower leaves to slow spread
Mulch to prevent soil splash onto leaves
Avoid working in wet fields
Practice crop rotation
Destroy infected plant residues after harvest

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Two-spotted spider mites (Tetranychus urticae) are tiny arachnids that suck plant sap, causing stippling and bronzing of leaves.
symptoms: Tiny yellow or white speckles (stippling) on upper leaf surface
Fine webbing on leaf undersides and between leaves
Leaves turn bronze, dry out, and drop
Stunted plant growth in severe infestations
prevention: Monitor regularly with hand lens â€” check leaf undersides
Maintain adequate plant moisture â€” mites thrive in dry, dusty conditions
Encourage natural predators (ladybugs, predatory mites)
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Spray water on leaf undersides to dislodge mites
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill beneficial predators

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target spot is caused by Corynespora cassiicola. It produces large, concentric-ringed lesions on tomato leaves, stems, and fruit.
symptoms: Small, brown spots that enlarge with concentric rings
Spots coalesce causing large areas of dead tissue
Can affect stems and fruit
Premature defoliation
prevention: Use resistant varieties when available
Ensure proper plant spacing
Remove and destroy infected plant material
Practice crop rotation
Avoid overhead irrigation

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: TYLCV is transmitted by the whitefly Bemisia tabaci. It causes severe stunting and yield loss in tomatoes.
symptoms: Severe upward curling and cupping of leaves
Leaves become small and yellow
Stunted plant growth
Flowers drop â€” fruit production is severely reduced or absent
Plant may appear bushy from shortened internodes
pesticides: No cure for virus â€” control the whitefly vector
Neem oil 1500 ppm (5 mL/L) as repellent
prevention: Use TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties
Install yellow sticky traps to monitor whiteflies
Use UV-reflective mulch to deter whiteflies
Cover nurseries with insect-proof net (40 mesh)
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Avoid planting tomatoes near heavily infested fields

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: Tomato mosaic virus is a highly stable virus spread mechanically through contaminated tools, hands, and infected seed.
symptoms: Light and dark green mosaic pattern on leaves
Leaf distortion, curling, or fern-like appearance
Stunted growth
Yellowish mottling on fruit
Reduced fruit size and quality
pesticides: No chemical treatment available for viruses
Focus on prevention and vector control
prevention: Use certified virus-free seed
Wash hands with soap and water before handling plants
Disinfect tools with 10% bleach or milk solution
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Do not use tobacco products while working with tomatoes
Plant resistant varieties (those with Tm-2 gene)

Tomato___healthy.description: The tomato plant appears healthy with no visible signs of disease.
symptoms: No symptoms detected
prevention: Stake or cage plants for support and air circulation
Water at the base, not overhead
Mulch to prevent soil splash and conserve moisture
Remove suckers for better fruit production
Monitor regularly for early signs of pests and diseases

Blueberry___Mummy_berry.description: Mummy berry is caused by the fungus Monilinia vaccinii-corymbosi. It is the most economically important disease of blueberries, causing shoot blight and fruit mummification.
symptoms: Sudden wilting and browning of new shoots and flower clusters
Infected berries appear normal initially but turn tan, then gray and shriveled
Mummified berries fall to ground and overwinter
Brown discoloration at base of infected shoots
prevention: Rake and remove mummified berries from under bushes
Apply 2-3 inches of fresh mulch to bury mummies
Plant resistant varieties when available
Apply fungicide at bud break and during bloom
Ensure good air circulation through pruning

Blueberry___Botrytis_blight.description: Botrytis blight is caused by Botrytis cinerea. It affects flowers and fruit, causing gray mold especially during cool, wet weather.
symptoms: Gray fuzzy mold on flowers and developing fruit
Blighted blossoms turn brown and cling to stems
Fruit rots with gray mold covering
Shoot tips may wilt and die back
prevention: Ensure good air circulation through pruning
Avoid overhead irrigation during bloom
Harvest fruit promptly when ripe
Remove dead flowers and infected fruit
Maintain proper plant spacing

Blueberry___Anthracnose.description: Anthracnose fruit rot is caused by Colletotrichum acutatum. It causes soft rot of ripe fruit, especially in warm, wet conditions.
symptoms: Soft, sunken lesions on ripe or ripening fruit
Orange spore masses on fruit surface in humid conditions
Fruit shrivels and drops prematurely
Infections can remain latent until fruit ripens
prevention: Prune to improve air circulation and light penetration
Harvest fruit frequently and promptly
Avoid overhead irrigation
Remove infected fruit and debris from field
Apply fungicides from bloom through harvest

Raspberry___Anthracnose.description: Raspberry anthracnose is caused by Elsinoe veneta. It produces sunken, gray-centered lesions on canes, leaves, and fruit.
symptoms: Small, purplish spots on young canes
Spots enlarge to oval, sunken lesions with gray centers and purple borders
Bark may crack at lesion sites
Fruit may be small, dry, and seedy
pesticides: Copper hydroxide 77% WP (2 g/L) — dormant spray
prevention: Remove and destroy old fruiting canes after harvest
Thin canes to improve air circulation
Avoid overhead irrigation
Apply dormant spray of lime sulfur or copper
Plant resistant varieties

Raspberry___Late_leaf_rust.description: Late leaf rust is caused by Pucciniastrum americanum. It causes premature defoliation and weakens canes.
symptoms: Small yellow pustules on lower leaf surfaces
Upper leaf surface shows yellow spots
Premature leaf drop in late summer
Weakened canes produce less fruit the following year
prevention: Remove wild brambles near cultivated plantings
Ensure good air circulation through proper spacing
Remove fallen leaves in autumn
Apply fungicide when rust first appears
Plant resistant varieties

Raspberry___Botrytis_fruit_rot.description: Gray mold caused by Botrytis cinerea is the most common post-harvest disease of raspberries, causing soft rot with gray fuzzy mold.
symptoms: Soft, light brown rot on ripe fruit
Gray fuzzy mold covering affected berries
Fruit leaks juice and collapses
Disease spreads rapidly to adjacent berries
prevention: Harvest fruit frequently in dry conditions
Ensure good air circulation through cane thinning
Avoid overhead irrigation during fruiting
Remove and destroy infected fruit promptly
Cool harvested fruit immediately to 0-2°C

Soybean___Rust.description: Asian soybean rust is caused by Phakopsora pachyrhizi. It is one of the most destructive diseases of soybean worldwide, capable of causing 80% yield loss.
symptoms: Small, tan to dark brown lesions on lower leaves
Lesions produce tan to reddish-brown pustules on leaf undersides
Rapid yellowing and premature defoliation
Pods may also develop lesions in severe cases
prevention: Plant early-maturing varieties to escape peak rust season
Monitor fields regularly from flowering stage
Apply fungicide at first sign of rust
Avoid late planting
Destroy volunteer soybean plants

Soybean___Septoria_brown_spot.description: Septoria brown spot is caused by Septoria glycines. It is one of the most common foliar diseases of soybeans.
symptoms: Small, irregular, dark brown spots on lower leaves
Spots may merge causing large necrotic areas
Premature yellowing and defoliation of lower leaves
Usually starts on unifoliate and lower trifoliate leaves
prevention: Rotate with non-host crops like corn or wheat
Bury crop residues through tillage
Use resistant varieties
Maintain balanced fertility
Apply foliar fungicide if disease appears before pod fill

Soybean___Bacterial_blight.description: Bacterial blight is caused by Pseudomonas savastanoi pv. glycinea. It causes angular leaf spots, especially after storms with wind-driven rain.
symptoms: Small, angular, water-soaked spots on leaves
Spots turn yellow-brown to dark brown with yellow halos
Centers of spots may dry and fall out
Symptoms appear on upper canopy after storms
pesticides: No highly effective chemical controls — focus on prevention
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed
Rotate crops for at least one year
Avoid working in fields when foliage is wet
Plant resistant varieties
Bury infected crop residues

Rice___Blast.description: Rice blast is caused by Magnaporthe oryzae. It is the most important disease of rice worldwide, attacking leaves, nodes, and panicles.
symptoms: Diamond-shaped or elliptical spots with gray centers and brown margins on leaves
Spots may coalesce to kill entire leaves
Neck rot causing panicle to break and hang (neck blast)
Node blackening leading to stem breakage
prevention: Use blast-resistant varieties
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Maintain proper water management in paddies
Treat seeds with fungicide before sowing
Remove and destroy infected crop residues
Maintain proper spacing between plants

Rice___Brown_spot.description: Brown spot is caused by Bipolaris oryzae (Cochliobolus miyabeanus). It causes oval brown spots on leaves and grain discoloration.
symptoms: Oval to circular brown spots with gray centers on leaves
Spots surrounded by yellow halo
Grain discoloration affecting quality
Seedling blight if seed is infected
prevention: Use certified seed and treat with fungicide
Maintain balanced nutrition especially potassium and silicon
Ensure proper water management
Remove infected crop residues
Use resistant varieties

Rice___Bacterial_leaf_blight.description: Bacterial leaf blight is caused by Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae. It is one of the most serious diseases of rice in Asia.
symptoms: Water-soaked streaks along leaf veins
Streaks enlarge and turn yellow to white
Leaves dry up from tips and margins
Milky bacterial ooze on leaf surface in early morning
pesticides: No highly effective chemical cure — resistant varieties preferred
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (recommended by IRRI/local stations)
Avoid clipping seedling tips during transplanting
Drain fields during severe outbreaks
Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization
Use balanced NPK with extra potassium

Rice___Sheath_blight.description: Sheath blight is caused by Rhizoctonia solani. It attacks leaf sheaths near water level, causing oval lesions that expand upward.
symptoms: Oval or irregular greenish-gray lesions on leaf sheaths near water line
Lesions enlarge and merge, moving upward
Affected sheaths and leaves dry up and turn brown
White mycelial growth visible on lesions in humid conditions
prevention: Avoid dense planting — maintain proper spacing
Manage nitrogen application carefully
Drain water periodically to lower humidity
Use seed treatment with Trichoderma viride
Remove sclerotia from fields before planting

Wheat___Rust_stripe.description: Stripe rust is caused by Puccinia striiformis f. sp. tritici. It forms yellow-orange pustules arranged in stripes along leaf veins.
symptoms: Yellow to orange pustules arranged in long stripes between leaf veins
Leaves turn yellow and dry up
Green islands may remain between stripe patterns
Spores released in powdery masses
prevention: Plant resistant varieties recommended for your region
Timely sowing to avoid peak rust season
Monitor fields regularly from tillering stage
Apply fungicide at first sign of rust
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization

Wheat___Leaf_rust.description: Leaf rust is caused by Puccinia triticina. It is the most common rust disease of wheat, producing small, round, orange-brown pustules on leaves.
symptoms: Small, round to oval, orange-brown pustules scattered on leaf surface
Pustules mostly on upper leaf surface
Premature leaf drying in severe infections
Reduced grain filling and shriveled kernels
prevention: Grow rust-resistant varieties
Apply fungicides at first appearance of pustules
Avoid late sowing
Maintain balanced fertilization
Destroy volunteer wheat plants

Wheat___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew of wheat is caused by Blumeria graminis f. sp. tritici. It produces white powdery patches on leaves and stems.
symptoms: White to grayish powdery patches on leaves, stems, and heads
Patches turn gray-brown as they age
Leaves may yellow and die prematurely
Reduced tillering and grain fill
prevention: Use resistant varieties
Avoid excessive nitrogen application
Ensure proper plant spacing
Apply foliar fungicide at first sign
Avoid dense sowing

Cotton___Bacterial_blight.description: Bacterial blight is caused by Xanthomonas citri pv. malvacearum. It causes angular water-soaked lesions on leaves, stems, and bolls.
symptoms: Small, angular, water-soaked spots on leaves between veins
Spots turn brown to black with age
Black arm symptom — dark lesions on stems and petioles
Boll rot with sunken, dark spots on immature bolls
pesticides: Seed treatment with Carboxin 37.5% + Thiram 37.5% DS (3 g/kg seed)
prevention: Use acid-delinted, treated seed
Plant resistant varieties (consult local Krishi Vigyan Kendra)
Practice crop rotation with non-host crops
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Avoid working in fields when foliage is wet

Cotton___Bollworm_damage.description: American bollworm (Helicoverpa armigera) is the most destructive pest of cotton in India. Larvae bore into bolls and feed on developing seeds and lint.
symptoms: Bore holes in squares, flowers, and bolls
Frass (excrement) at entry points
Damaged bolls may rot or drop prematurely
Larvae visible inside open bolls
pesticides: Neem oil 1500 ppm (5 mL/L) — for early instars
prevention: Install pheromone traps to monitor adult moths
Use Bt cotton varieties (approved GM varieties)
Intercrop with trap crops like marigold or pigeon pea
Release Trichogramma parasitoid wasps
Practice crop rotation and destroy crop stubble after harvest

Cotton___Leaf_curl_virus.description: Cotton leaf curl virus is transmitted by the whitefly Bemisia tabaci. It is a devastating disease in Indian and Pakistani cotton regions.
symptoms: Upward or downward curling of leaves
Thickening and darkening of leaf veins (enations)
Leaf-like outgrowths on underside of leaves
Stunted plant growth and reduced boll formation
pesticides: No cure for virus — control the whitefly vector
prevention: Plant CLCuV-resistant or tolerant varieties
Early sowing to escape peak whitefly period
Install yellow sticky traps for whitefly monitoring
Uproot and destroy infected plants
Avoid growing alternate whitefly hosts nearby

Groundnut___Tikka_leaf_spot.display_name: Groundnut Tikka Disease (Early & Late Leaf Spot)
Groundnut___Tikka_leaf_spot.description: Tikka disease includes early leaf spot (Cercospora arachidicola) and late leaf spot (Cercosporidium personatum). These are the most common diseases of groundnut in India.
symptoms: Dark brown circular spots on upper leaf surface (early leaf spot)
Black spots mainly on lower leaf surface (late leaf spot)
Severe defoliation reducing pod yield
Dark lesions on petioles and stems
prevention: Use resistant varieties (ICGV series)
Treat seeds with Thiram or Carbendazim
Remove and destroy crop debris
Practice crop rotation (2-3 years)
Apply foliar fungicides at 30-35 days after sowing

Groundnut___Rust.description: Groundnut rust is caused by Puccinia arachidis. It produces orange-brown pustules on leaf undersides.
symptoms: Small, orange-brown to reddish-brown pustules on lower leaf surface
Corresponding light spots on upper surface
Severe infection causes premature defoliation
Reduced pod and kernel development
prevention: Plant resistant varieties
Timely sowing to avoid peak disease period
Remove volunteer groundnut plants
Apply foliar fungicide at disease onset
Practice crop rotation with cereals

Sugarcane___Red_rot.description: Red rot is caused by Colletotrichum falcatum. It is the most serious disease of sugarcane in India, causing internal red discoloration of the stalk.
symptoms: Yellowing and drying of leaves from tip downward
Red discoloration inside cane when split open
White patches within red-rotted tissue (diagnostic feature)
Shrunken and hollowed canes
Alcohol-like smell from infected canes
pesticides: Sett treatment with Trichoderma viride (10 g/L for 15 min)
prevention: Use disease-free seed cane (setts) from registered nurseries
Treat setts with fungicide before planting
Plant resistant varieties (consult local cane research station)
Practice crop rotation with non-host crops
Rogue out and destroy infected clumps immediately

Sugarcane___Smut.description: Smut of sugarcane is caused by Sporisorium scitamineum. It produces a distinctive black whip-like structure from the growing point.
symptoms: Long, black whip-like structure emerging from the top of the cane
Whip composed of black spore mass covered by thin membrane
Thin, pencil-like tillers (grass-like shoots)
Reduced cane thickness and sugar content
pesticides: Seed treatment with Propiconazole 25% EC (1 mL/L) for 15 min
Hot water treatment of setts at 52°C for 30 minutes
Carbendazim 50% WP — sett dip (1 g/L for 15 min)
prevention: Use smut-free setts from disease-free nurseries
Hot water treatment of seed cane at 52°C for 30 minutes
Remove and burn smut whips before they open and release spores
Rogue out infected clumps from the field
Plant resistant varieties

Chilli___Leaf_curl_virus.description: Chilli leaf curl is caused by a begomovirus transmitted by the whitefly Bemisia tabaci. It causes severe curling and puckering of leaves.
symptoms: Upward curling and puckering of leaves
Leaves become small and deformed
Internode shortening giving bushy appearance
Drastic reduction in fruit set and yield
pesticides: No cure for virus — control the whitefly vector
prevention: Use virus-free nursery seedlings
Grow under insect-proof net (40 mesh) in nursery
Install yellow sticky traps to monitor whiteflies
Uproot and destroy infected plants early
Avoid growing chilli near cotton or tomato

Chilli___Anthracnose_fruit_rot.description: Anthracnose of chilli is caused by Colletotrichum capsici and related species. It causes fruit rot and die-back of branches.
symptoms: Sunken, dark, circular lesions on ripe fruit
Concentric rings of dark fruiting bodies on lesions
Tip die-back of branches progressing downward
Flower drop and reduced fruit set
prevention: Use disease-free seeds from healthy fruits
Treat seeds with Thiram or Carbendazim
Practice crop rotation (2-3 years)
Avoid overhead irrigation during fruiting
Collect and destroy infected fruits from field

Chilli___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew of chilli is caused by Leveillula taurica. It produces white powdery patches mainly on the lower leaf surface.
symptoms: White powdery patches on lower leaf surface
Yellow spots on corresponding upper surface
Severe defoliation exposing fruit to sunscald
Reduced fruit size and quality
pesticides: Sulphur 80% WP (3 g/L) — avoid in hot weather above 35°C
prevention: Use resistant varieties
Ensure adequate spacing for air circulation
Avoid water stress
Apply sulfur-based fungicide preventively
Remove severely infected leaves

Mango___Anthracnose.description: Anthracnose is caused by Colletotrichum gloeosporioides. It is the most important pre- and post-harvest disease of mango worldwide.
symptoms: Black spots on flowers causing blossom blight
Dark, sunken lesions on fruit
Leaf spots and twig die-back
Post-harvest black decay of ripe fruit
pesticides: Hot water treatment of fruit at 52°C for 5 min (post-harvest)
prevention: Spray fungicide at panicle emergence and again at fruit set
Prune canopy for better air circulation and light
Remove dead wood and mummified fruit
Harvest fruit carefully to avoid wounds
Hot water dip of fruit after harvest to control latent infections

Mango___Powdery_mildew.description: Powdery mildew of mango is caused by Oidium mangiferae. It affects flowers, young fruit, and leaves, causing heavy flower and fruit drop.
symptoms: White powdery coating on flowers, panicles, and young leaves
Flower and fruitlet drop
Malformed and stunted fruit
Distorted young leaves
prevention: Spray sulphur at panicle emergence (before flowering)
Repeat spray during full bloom if weather is cool and humid
Prune trees for proper air circulation
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Remove and destroy affected panicles

Banana___Panama_wilt.description: Panama disease is caused by Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense. It is one of the most destructive diseases of banana worldwide, causing total plant death.
symptoms: Yellowing of older leaves starting from margins
Leaves collapse and hang around the pseudostem (skirt-like)
Longitudinal splitting of pseudostem base
Reddish-brown discoloration in vascular tissue when pseudostem is cut
pesticides: Carbendazim 50% WP (2 g/L) — soil drench for suppression
Trichoderma viride (50 g/plant as bio-control agent)
prevention: Use disease-free tissue culture plantlets
Practice crop rotation (3-5 years with rice or sugarcane)
Avoid moving soil from infested to clean fields
Treat rhizomes with fungicide + bio-agents before planting
Maintain soil pH above 6.0
Do not use furrow irrigation from infested areas

Banana___Sigatoka_leaf_spot.description: Sigatoka leaf spot includes Yellow Sigatoka (Mycosphaerella musicola) and Black Sigatoka (M. fijiensis). These are the most economically important leaf diseases of banana.
symptoms: Yellow streaks on leaves that turn brown with gray centers
Streaks enlarge into oval spots with dark borders
Dark brown to black lesions (Black Sigatoka)
Premature drying and death of large leaf areas
Premature fruit ripening and reduced bunch weight
pesticides: Mineral oil (agricultural grade) + fungicide for better coverage
prevention: De-leaf infected leaves and remove from field
Maintain proper spacing between plants
Ensure good drainage to reduce humidity
Apply fungicide before monsoon season
Use resistant varieties (Poovan, Monthan)

Onion___Purple_blotch.description: Purple blotch is caused by Alternaria porri. It is the most important foliar disease of onion in India.
symptoms: Small, sunken, whitish spots that enlarge into purple-brown lesions
Lesions develop concentric rings
Affected leaves bend and break at lesion point
Severe defoliation reducing bulb size
pesticides: Add sticker (Teepol) at 1 mL/L for better adherence
prevention: Practice crop rotation (2-3 years)
Use healthy bulbs for planting
Maintain proper plant spacing for air circulation
Avoid overhead irrigation
Start protective fungicide sprays before symptoms appear

Mustard___White_rust.description: White rust is caused by Albugo candida. It produces white blister-like pustules on leaves, stems, and inflorescence of mustard.
symptoms: White, shiny, blister-like pustules on lower leaf surface
Corresponding yellow spots on upper surface
Distorted and swollen inflorescence (staghead symptom)
Flowers become sterile — no seed formation in affected heads
pesticides: Seed treatment with Metalaxyl 35% WS (6 g/kg seed)
prevention: Use resistant varieties
Treat seeds with Metalaxyl before sowing
Practice crop rotation with non-cruciferous crops
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Timely sowing to avoid peak disease period

Turmeric___Rhizome_rot.description: Rhizome rot is caused by Pythium aphanidermatum. It is the most destructive disease of turmeric, causing complete rotting of the underground rhizome.
symptoms: Yellowing and wilting of lower leaves
Soft, watery rot of rhizome
Foul smell from infected rhizome
Collar region becomes soft and water-soaked
Plants pull out easily from soil
pesticides: Metalaxyl 8% + Mancozeb 64% WP (Ridomil Gold — 2.5 g/L) — soil drench
Copper oxychloride 50% WP (3 g/L) — soil drench
Rhizome treatment with Mancozeb 75% WP (3 g/L) for 30 min before planting
prevention: Select healthy, disease-free seed rhizomes
Treat rhizomes with fungicide + bio-agent before planting
Ensure good soil drainage
Practice crop rotation (3 years minimum)
Apply Trichoderma viride enriched FYM at planting
Drench soil with fungicide at first sign of wilting
